1:
"And it came to pass, when all the kings of the Amorites... heard that the LORD had dried up the waters of Jordan from before the children of Israel... that their heart melted, neither was there spirit in them any more." The witnesses note the hearts of the Canaanite kings melting at the news of Jordan dried up before Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were the kings on the west side of Jordan, distinct from Sihon and Og already conquered. The witnesses note that their heart melted and there was no spirit left in them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the kings heard — of Jordan dried up; the the LORD's mighty act — terrifying the foe; the their heart melted — no spirit left; the God breaking their courage — no courage remaining (Josh. 2:11).

The witnesses agree the drying of Jordan struck terror into the enemies of God's people before a blow was struck: when the Canaanite kings "heard that the LORD had dried up the waters," their "heart melted, neither was there spirit in them"; the very rumour of God's mighty act unmanned the foe, as Rahab had foretold; it teaches that God goes before his people to break the courage of their enemies, the fear of the LORD falling on the wicked, "the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast... and upon all the inhabitants" (cf. Josh. 2:11, neither did there remain any more courage), the conquest begun in the melting of their hearts.

So the witnesses make the verse their heart melted: The witnesses note the the kings heard: of Jordan dried up. The witnesses note the the LORD's mighty act: terrifying the foe. The witnesses note the their heart melted: no spirit left. The witnesses note the God breaking their courage: no courage remaining (Josh. 2:11). The expositors observe the the kings heard: of Jordan dried up. The expositors observe the the LORD's mighty act: terrifying the foe. The expositors observe the their heart melted: no spirit left. The expositors observe the God breaking their courage: no courage remaining (Josh. 2:11). The believer is taught to mark the the kings heard, the the LORD's mighty act, the their heart melted, the God breaking their courage, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that the Canaanite kings' hearts melted at the drying of Jordan — God going before to break the courage of his people's enemies, the conquest begun in the melting of their hearts (Josh. 2:11).
2:
"At that time the LORD said unto Joshua, Make thee sharp knives, and circumcise again the children of Israel the second time." The witnesses note God commanding the renewal of circumcision, neglected through the wilderness years. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was after they had crossed Jordan and pitched at Gilgal, when the dread had seized Canaan — a fit time for the command. The witnesses note that they were to be circumcised again, the second time. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the make thee sharp knives — the rite prepared; the circumcise again — the covenant-sign renewed; the the second time — neglected in the wilderness; the marked as God's own — the token of the covenant (Gen. 17:11).

The witnesses agree the first act in the land was the renewal of the covenant-sign: God bade Joshua "circumcise again" the people, restoring the rite neglected during the forty years' wandering; the timing — just after crossing Jordan and before the conquest — shows that the people must be marked afresh as God's own before they went up to possess the land; it teaches that the covenant-seal must be in order before God's blessing is enjoyed, the people consecrated by the sign of the covenant, circumcision the token of belonging to the LORD (cf. Gen. 17:11), renewed at the threshold of the inheritance.

So the witnesses make the verse circumcise again: The witnesses note the make thee sharp knives: the rite prepared. The witnesses note the circumcise again: the covenant-sign renewed. The witnesses note the the second time: neglected in the wilderness. The witnesses note the marked as God's own: the token of the covenant (Gen. 17:11). The expositors observe the make thee sharp knives: the rite prepared. The expositors observe the circumcise again: the covenant-sign renewed. The expositors observe the the second time: neglected in the wilderness. The expositors observe the marked as God's own: the token of the covenant (Gen. 17:11). The believer is taught to mark the make thee sharp knives, the circumcise again, the the second time, the marked as God's own, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that God bade Joshua circumcise the people again — the covenant-sign renewed at the threshold of the land, the people marked afresh as God's own before the conquest (Gen. 17:11).
3:
"And Joshua made him sharp knives, and circumcised the children of Israel at the hill of the foreskins." The witnesses note Joshua causing the people to be circumcised at the appointed place. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua did not circumcise them himself, any more than he made the knives himself, but ordered and saw it done; circumcision was not restricted to one sort of person. The witnesses note that it was done at the hill of the foreskins. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the made sharp knives — the rite prepared; the circumcised the children — by Joshua's order; the the hill of the foreskins — the completeness of the act; the the neglect set right — restored to the covenant-mark (Col. 2:11).

The witnesses agree the command was promptly and fully obeyed: Joshua "circumcised the children of Israel" — not with his own hand only, Gill notes, but by his order and oversight, the whole people brought under the rite; the naming of the place "the hill of the foreskins" marks the completeness of the act, the whole uncircumcised generation now sealed; it teaches the obedience that loses no time in setting right what has been neglected, the people restored to the covenant-mark before they advanced, the sign of consecration borne by all (cf. Col. 2:11, the circumcision made without hands).

So the witnesses make the verse circumcised the people: The witnesses note the made sharp knives: the rite prepared. The witnesses note the circumcised the children: by Joshua's order. The witnesses note the the hill of the foreskins: the completeness of the act. The witnesses note the the neglect set right: restored to the covenant-mark (Col. 2:11). The expositors observe the made sharp knives: the rite prepared. The expositors observe the circumcised the children: by Joshua's order. The expositors observe the the hill of the foreskins: the completeness of the act. The expositors observe the the neglect set right: restored to the covenant-mark (Col. 2:11). The believer is taught to mark the made sharp knives, the circumcised the children, the the hill of the foreskins, the the neglect set right, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that Joshua had the people circumcised at the appointed place — prompt obedience setting right the neglect, the whole generation sealed with the covenant-mark before they advanced (Col. 2:11).
4:
"And this is the cause why Joshua did circumcise: All the people that came out of Egypt, that were males... died in the wilderness by the way, after they came out of Egypt." The witnesses note the reason — the men who left Egypt had died in the wilderness, and the new generation needed the rite. The witnesses note that Gill notes the reason of the command, namely what follows. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set all the males that came out of Egypt having died in the wilderness. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the cause of it — the reason of the renewal; the the men of Egypt — all the males who left; the died in the wilderness — the judged generation gone; the a new people sealed — the carcases fallen, the children marked (Heb. 3:17).

The witnesses agree the cause of the renewal lay in the judgment that had fallen on the first generation: "all the people that came out of Egypt... died in the wilderness," and so the rite had lapsed and must be restored to their children; the explanation joins the renewal of the sign to the passing of the unbelieving generation, a new people taking up the covenant the fathers had forfeited; it teaches that the judgment on unbelief left a generation to be marked afresh, the covenant carried on to the children when the fathers fell, "their carcases fell in the wilderness" (Heb. 3:17), the sign renewed in the new generation.

So the witnesses make the verse the cause: The witnesses note the the cause of it: the reason of the renewal. The witnesses note the the men of Egypt: all the males who left. The witnesses note the died in the wilderness: the judged generation gone. The witnesses note the a new people sealed: the carcases fallen, the children marked (Heb. 3:17). The expositors observe the the cause of it: the reason of the renewal. The expositors observe the the men of Egypt: all the males who left. The expositors observe the died in the wilderness: the judged generation gone. The expositors observe the a new people sealed: the carcases fallen, the children marked (Heb. 3:17). The believer is taught to mark the the cause of it, the the men of Egypt, the died in the wilderness, the a new people sealed, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that the cause of the renewal was the death of the Egypt-generation in the wilderness — the covenant carried on to the children when the fathers fell, the sign renewed in the new generation (Heb. 3:17).
5:
"Now all the people that came out were circumcised: but all the people that were born in the wilderness... them they had not circumcised." The witnesses note the contrast — the Egypt-generation were circumcised, but the wilderness-born were not. The witnesses note that Gill notes all that came out of Egypt, males, were circumcised, whether under or above twenty; the wilderness-born had not been. The witnesses note that those born in the wandering were uncircumcised. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came out circumcised — the Egypt-generation; the born in the wilderness — the new generation; the not circumcised — the rite suspended; the the sign withheld — the people under chastening (Num. 14:33).

The witnesses agree the verse marks the long lapse of the rite during the wandering: the generation that left Egypt had been circumcised, but "all the people that were born in the wilderness... them they had not circumcised"; for the forty years of judgment the covenant-sign had been suspended, a token that the people were under chastening and out of full covenant favour; it teaches that the lapse of the sign answered the breach of the covenant in that generation, the seal withheld while the people wandered under sentence, now restored as a new day dawned (cf. Num. 14:33, your children shall wander).

So the witnesses make the verse the wilderness-born: The witnesses note the came out circumcised: the Egypt-generation. The witnesses note the born in the wilderness: the new generation. The witnesses note the not circumcised: the rite suspended. The witnesses note the the sign withheld: the people under chastening (Num. 14:33). The expositors observe the came out circumcised: the Egypt-generation. The expositors observe the born in the wilderness: the new generation. The expositors observe the not circumcised: the rite suspended. The expositors observe the the sign withheld: the people under chastening (Num. 14:33). The believer is taught to mark the came out circumcised, the born in the wilderness, the not circumcised, the the sign withheld, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that the wilderness-born had not been circumcised — the covenant-sign suspended through the years of judgment, the seal withheld while the people wandered under sentence, now restored (Num. 14:33).
6:
"For the children of Israel walked forty years in the wilderness... unto whom the LORD sware that he would not shew them the land... a land that floweth with milk and honey." The witnesses note the forty years' wandering, the LORD having sworn the first generation should not see the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes the forty years, wanting a few days, the round number given. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the first person "us" used, the passage knitting the wilderness story to the entrance. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the forty years — in the wilderness; the the LORD sware — the first generation barred; the not see the land — for their unbelief; the the oath fulfilled — they should not enter my rest (Ps. 95:11).

The witnesses agree the verse rehearses the long judgment that occasioned the lapse of the sign: "forty years in the wilderness" until the sworn-against generation was consumed, barred from the land of milk and honey for their unbelief; the recalling of the oath and the wandering grounds the present renewal in the close of that judgment; it teaches that God's threatenings are as sure as his promises, the rebellious generation kept from the rest by his oath, "so I sware in my wrath, that they should not enter into my rest" (Ps. 95:11), the wandering now ended and the sign restored.

So the witnesses make the verse forty years: The witnesses note the forty years: in the wilderness. The witnesses note the the LORD sware: the first generation barred. The witnesses note the not see the land: for their unbelief. The witnesses note the the oath fulfilled: they should not enter my rest (Ps. 95:11). The expositors observe the forty years: in the wilderness. The expositors observe the the LORD sware: the first generation barred. The expositors observe the not see the land: for their unbelief. The expositors observe the the oath fulfilled: they should not enter my rest (Ps. 95:11). The believer is taught to mark the forty years, the the LORD sware, the not see the land, the the oath fulfilled, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that Israel walked forty years in the wilderness, the sworn-against generation barred from the land — God's threatening as sure as his promise, the rebellious kept from the rest, the wandering now ended (Ps. 95:11).
7:
"And their children, whom he raised up in their stead, them Joshua circumcised: for they were uncircumcised, because they had not circumcised them by the way." The witnesses note the children, raised up in their fathers' stead, now circumcised by Joshua. The witnesses note that Gill notes those born in the wilderness, who succeeded the fathers, some near forty years old; those born before had been circumcised, but not these. The witnesses note that they had not been circumcised by the way. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the their children — raised up in their stead; the Joshua circumcised — the new generation sealed; the uncircumcised by the way — grown up in the wandering; the the covenant continued — the promise to the children (Acts 2:39).

The witnesses agree the renewal fell upon the new generation that God had raised up: "their children... them Joshua circumcised," for they had grown up uncircumcised through the wandering; the sealing of the children, now come to inherit, marks the fresh beginning of the covenant people in the land; it teaches that God preserves a seed to carry on his covenant when one generation has been judged, the children raised up in the fathers' stead and sealed with the covenant-mark, the promise continuing to the generation that goes in (cf. Acts 2:39, the promise is unto you, and to your children).

So the witnesses make the verse the children sealed: The witnesses note the their children: raised up in their stead. The witnesses note the Joshua circumcised: the new generation sealed. The witnesses note the uncircumcised by the way: grown up in the wandering. The witnesses note the the covenant continued: the promise to the children (Acts 2:39). The expositors observe the their children: raised up in their stead. The expositors observe the Joshua circumcised: the new generation sealed. The expositors observe the uncircumcised by the way: grown up in the wandering. The expositors observe the the covenant continued: the promise to the children (Acts 2:39). The believer is taught to mark the their children, the Joshua circumcised, the uncircumcised by the way, the the covenant continued, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that Joshua circumcised the children raised up in their fathers' stead — the fresh beginning of the covenant people in the land, the promise continuing to the generation that goes in (Acts 2:39).
8:
"And it came to pass, when they had done circumcising all the people, that they abode in their places in the camp, till they were whole." The witnesses note the people resting in the camp until healed of the circumcision. The witnesses note that Gill notes it seems done in one day; Bishop Usher thinks the day after, on the eleventh of Nisan. The witnesses note that they abode till they were whole. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the done circumcising all — the people obedient; the abode in the camp — resting in faith; the till they were whole — defenceless before the foe; the safe under God's keeping — he that keepeth thee slumbers not (Ps. 121:3).

The witnesses agree the people, having obeyed, waited in faith while they were healed: "they abode in their places... till they were whole," lying defenceless before Jericho in the very face of the enemy; the boldness of this — to render the fighting men sore and helpless on the edge of war — was an act of faith, trusting God to protect them while they kept his covenant; it teaches that obedience to God may set us in seeming danger, yet under his sure keeping, the people safe in their weakness because the LORD watched over them, "he that keepeth thee will not slumber" (Ps. 121:3), made whole under his guard.

So the witnesses make the verse till they were whole: The witnesses note the done circumcising all: the people obedient. The witnesses note the abode in the camp: resting in faith. The witnesses note the till they were whole: defenceless before the foe. The witnesses note the safe under God's keeping: he that keepeth thee slumbers not (Ps. 121:3). The expositors observe the done circumcising all: the people obedient. The expositors observe the abode in the camp: resting in faith. The expositors observe the till they were whole: defenceless before the foe. The expositors observe the safe under God's keeping: he that keepeth thee slumbers not (Ps. 121:3). The believer is taught to mark the done circumcising all, the abode in the camp, the till they were whole, the safe under God's keeping, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that the people abode in the camp till healed — an act of faith, the fighting men helpless before Jericho yet safe under God's keeping, made whole under his guard (Ps. 121:3).
9:
"And the LORD said unto Joshua, This day have I rolled away the reproach of Egypt from off you. Wherefore the name of the place is called Gilgal unto this day." The witnesses note God declaring the reproach of Egypt rolled away, whence the place is named Gilgal. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD said this out of the tabernacle. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the reproach of Egypt rolled away when the circumcision was completed, the place named Gilgal (rolling). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rolled away — the reproach of Egypt; the from off you — the shame removed; the the name Gilgal — rolling, in memory; the reproach taken away — god hath taken away my reproach (Gen. 30:23).

The witnesses agree the renewal of the sign was crowned with a word of grace — the rolling away of reproach: "this day have I rolled away the reproach of Egypt from off you," the shame of their bondage and of the long uncircumcision removed, the place named Gilgal ("rolling") in memory; the restoring of the covenant-mark took away the reproach that had lain on them; it teaches that God removes the reproach of his people when they return to him, the shame of their former estate rolled away, "God hath taken away my reproach" (cf. Gen. 30:23), the people cleansed of the marks of Egypt.

So the witnesses make the verse the reproach rolled away: The witnesses note the rolled away: the reproach of Egypt. The witnesses note the from off you: the shame removed. The witnesses note the the name Gilgal: rolling, in memory. The witnesses note the reproach taken away: God hath taken away my reproach (Gen. 30:23). The expositors observe the rolled away: the reproach of Egypt. The expositors observe the from off you: the shame removed. The expositors observe the the name Gilgal: rolling, in memory. The expositors observe the reproach taken away: God hath taken away my reproach (Gen. 30:23). The believer is taught to mark the rolled away, the from off you, the the name Gilgal, the reproach taken away, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that God rolled away the reproach of Egypt, naming the place Gilgal — the shame of bondage and uncircumcision removed, God taking away the reproach of his people restored to him (Gen. 30:23).
10:
"And the children of Israel encamped in Gilgal, and kept the passover on the fourteenth day of the month at even in the plains of Jericho." The witnesses note Israel keeping the Passover at Gilgal, on the plains of Jericho. The witnesses note that Gill notes they encamped in Gilgal and kept the passover there, little more than a mile from Jericho. The witnesses note that it was on the fourteenth at even. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the encamped in Gilgal — near Jericho; the kept the passover — the feast of redemption; the on the fourteenth — the appointed day; the redemption remembered — christ our passover (1 Cor. 5:7-8).

The witnesses agree the keeping of the Passover marked their entrance into the land with the feast of redemption: in Gilgal, on the plains of Jericho, "they kept the passover on the fourteenth day," the first such feast in the promised land, recalling their deliverance from Egypt as they stood to possess their inheritance; the joining of the Passover to the conquest shows that the redeemed enter their rest remembering their redemption; it teaches that all God's gifts are received in the light of the great deliverance, the feast of the Lamb kept at the gate of the land, "Christ our passover is sacrificed for us; therefore let us keep the feast" (1 Cor. 5:7-8).

So the witnesses make the verse kept the passover: The witnesses note the encamped in Gilgal: near Jericho. The witnesses note the kept the passover: the feast of redemption. The witnesses note the on the fourteenth: the appointed day. The witnesses note the redemption remembered: Christ our passover (1 Cor. 5:7-8). The expositors observe the encamped in Gilgal: near Jericho. The expositors observe the kept the passover: the feast of redemption. The expositors observe the on the fourteenth: the appointed day. The expositors observe the redemption remembered: Christ our passover (1 Cor. 5:7-8). The believer is taught to mark the encamped in Gilgal, the kept the passover, the on the fourteenth, the redemption remembered, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that Israel kept the Passover at Gilgal on entering the land — the feast of redemption marking their possession, the redeemed entering their rest remembering their deliverance, Christ our passover (1 Cor. 5:7-8).
11:
"And they did eat of the old corn of the land on the morrow after the passover, unleavened cakes, and parched corn in the selfsame day." The witnesses note Israel eating the produce of the land — the old corn — after the Passover. The witnesses note that Gill notes the old corn of the last year, which they might eat, the new not lawful until the wave sheaf was offered. The witnesses note that they ate unleavened cakes and parched corn that same day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the old corn — of the land; the after the passover — the produce eaten; the unleavened cakes — and parched corn; the provision suited to their state — the manna giving way to the corn (Josh. 5:12).

The witnesses agree the eating of the land's produce marked a new stage in God's provision: now in Canaan, they "did eat of the old corn of the land," the fruit of the promised inheritance taking the place of the wilderness manna; the transition from miraculous to ordinary supply shows God's care passing from one form to another as his people's estate changed; it teaches that God feeds his people by means suited to their condition, the wilderness manna giving way to the corn of the land, the gift the same though the manner differs, the LORD still their provider in the land of promise (cf. Josh. 5:12).

So the witnesses make the verse the corn of the land: The witnesses note the the old corn: of the land. The witnesses note the after the passover: the produce eaten. The witnesses note the unleavened cakes: and parched corn. The witnesses note the provision suited to their state: the manna giving way to the corn (Josh. 5:12). The expositors observe the the old corn: of the land. The expositors observe the after the passover: the produce eaten. The expositors observe the unleavened cakes: and parched corn. The expositors observe the provision suited to their state: the manna giving way to the corn (Josh. 5:12). The believer is taught to mark the the old corn, the after the passover, the unleavened cakes, the provision suited to their state, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that Israel ate the old corn of the land after the Passover — God's provision passing from the wilderness manna to the fruit of the inheritance, the gift the same though the manner differs (Josh. 5:12).
12:
"And the manna ceased on the morrow after they had eaten of the old corn of the land; neither had the children of Israel manna any more; but they did eat of the fruit of the land of Canaan that year." The witnesses note the manna ceasing once Israel ate the produce of Canaan. The witnesses note that Gill notes there was now no further need of it; miracles are not continued when unnecessary; the manna, a type of Christ, ceased. The witnesses note that they ate the fruit of the land that year. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the manna ceased — the miraculous bread ended; the no more manna — the wilderness supply done; the the fruit of the land — the ordinary means begun; the the type fulfilled — the true bread from heaven (John 6:32).

The witnesses agree the ceasing of the manna marked the close of one age of provision and the entrance on another: "the manna ceased... but they did eat of the fruit of the land," the miraculous bread no longer given once the land supplied them; Gill notes the manna was "a type of Christ," and its ceasing as the ordinary food began may shadow the passing of the figure into the substance; it teaches that God's extraordinary helps give way when the ordinary means are at hand, yet the manna's deeper meaning abides in Christ, "the true bread from heaven" (John 6:32), the type fulfilled though the sign ceased.

So the witnesses make the verse the manna ceased: The witnesses note the the manna ceased: the miraculous bread ended. The witnesses note the no more manna: the wilderness supply done. The witnesses note the the fruit of the land: the ordinary means begun. The witnesses note the the type fulfilled: the true bread from heaven (John 6:32). The expositors observe the the manna ceased: the miraculous bread ended. The expositors observe the no more manna: the wilderness supply done. The expositors observe the the fruit of the land: the ordinary means begun. The expositors observe the the type fulfilled: the true bread from heaven (John 6:32). The believer is taught to mark the the manna ceased, the no more manna, the the fruit of the land, the the type fulfilled, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that the manna ceased when Israel ate the fruit of Canaan — the extraordinary giving way to the ordinary, yet the manna a type of Christ, the true bread from heaven, the figure abiding though the sign ceased (John 6:32).
13:
"And it came to pass, when Joshua was by Jericho, that he lifted up his eyes and looked, and, behold, there stood a man over against him with his sword drawn in his hand: and Joshua went unto him, and said unto him, Art thou for us, or for our adversaries?" The witnesses note Joshua meeting a man with a drawn sword near Jericho, and asking whose side he was on. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua was on the border or side of Jericho, reconnoitring alone, very probably seeking where to make his first attack. The witnesses note that he saw a man with sword drawn and asked, Art thou for us, or for our adversaries? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joshua by Jericho — reconnoitring alone; the a man with sword drawn — one come for war; the Joshua went unto him — his boldness; the for us or adversaries — a greater Captain at hand (Josh. 5:14).

The witnesses agree the scene opens with a mysterious and majestic figure appearing to Joshua on the eve of battle: "a man... with his sword drawn," whom Joshua, taking for a warrior, challenges, "art thou for us, or for our adversaries?"; the drawn sword marks one come for war, and Joshua's boldness in approaching shows his courage; the question, soon answered, prepares for the revealing of one greater than a man; it teaches that God appears to strengthen his servants before their trials, the captain of the conquest met by a greater Captain at the threshold of the war, the drawn sword a token that the battle is the Lord's (cf. Josh. 5:14).

So the witnesses make the verse the man with the sword: The witnesses note the Joshua by Jericho: reconnoitring alone. The witnesses note the a man with sword drawn: one come for war. The witnesses note the Joshua went unto him: his boldness. The witnesses note the for us or adversaries: a greater Captain at hand (Josh. 5:14). The expositors observe the Joshua by Jericho: reconnoitring alone. The expositors observe the a man with sword drawn: one come for war. The expositors observe the Joshua went unto him: his boldness. The expositors observe the for us or adversaries: a greater Captain at hand (Josh. 5:14). The believer is taught to mark the Joshua by Jericho, the a man with sword drawn, the Joshua went unto him, the for us or adversaries, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that Joshua met a man with a drawn sword near Jericho and asked whose side he was on — a majestic figure at the threshold of war, the captain of the conquest met by a greater Captain, the battle the Lord's.
14:
"And he said, Nay; but as captain of the host of the LORD am I now come. And Joshua fell on his face to the earth, and did worship, and said unto him, What saith my lord unto his servant?" The witnesses note the answer — he is the Captain of the LORD's host, whom Joshua worships. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was not come for or on the side of their adversaries, as Joshua suspected. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set him as captain of the host of the LORD, now come. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the captain of the host — of the LORD; the Joshua fell and worshipped — owning him divine; the worship received — as angels refuse; the Christ the Captain — the captain of their salvation (Heb. 2:10).

The witnesses agree the figure reveals himself as no mere man but the Captain of the LORD's host, and Joshua's worship marks him divine: "as captain of the host of the LORD am I now come," and Joshua "fell on his face... and did worship"; the receiving of worship, which angels refuse, shows this Captain to be a divine person — the pre-incarnate Christ, the Angel of the LORD, come to lead the conquest; Joshua's submission, "what saith my lord unto his servant?", owns him as Lord; it teaches that Christ himself is the Captain of his people's warfare, "the captain of their salvation" (Heb. 2:10), worshipped and obeyed as Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse the Captain of the LORD: The witnesses note the captain of the host: of the LORD. The witnesses note the Joshua fell and worshipped: owning him divine. The witnesses note the worship received: as angels refuse. The witnesses note the Christ the Captain: the captain of their salvation (Heb. 2:10). The expositors observe the captain of the host: of the LORD. The expositors observe the Joshua fell and worshipped: owning him divine. The expositors observe the worship received: as angels refuse. The expositors observe the Christ the Captain: the captain of their salvation (Heb. 2:10). The believer is taught to mark the captain of the host, the Joshua fell and worshipped, the worship received, the Christ the Captain, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that the figure was the Captain of the LORD's host, whom Joshua worshipped — a divine person, the pre-incarnate Christ leading the conquest, the Captain of his people's salvation, worshipped and obeyed (Heb. 2:10).
15:
"And the captain of the LORD's host said unto Joshua, Loose thy shoe from off thy foot; for the place whereon thou standest is holy. And Joshua did so." The witnesses note the command to put off the shoe, for the place is holy — the same word spoken to Moses at the bush. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was a trial and proof of Joshua's obedience. The witnesses note that Clarke notes these were the same words the angel spoke to Moses at Sinai (Exod. 3:5), so it seems likely it was the same Person. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the loose thy shoe — the command of reverence; the the place is holy — from his presence; the the same as at the bush — the same divine Person (Exod. 3:5); the Joshua did so — the reverence due him.

The witnesses agree the command "loose thy shoe... for the place is holy" confirms the Captain's divinity and identity: Clarke marks these as "the same words" spoken to Moses at the burning bush, so that this Captain "was the same Person" — the Angel of the LORD, the pre-incarnate Christ; the holiness of the ground flowed from his presence, as at the bush; Joshua's instant obedience, "and Joshua did so," shows the reverence due him; it teaches that wherever Christ is present is holy ground, demanding reverence and worship, the same divine Person meeting Joshua who had met Moses, "put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place... is holy ground" (Exod. 3:5).

So the witnesses make the verse holy ground: The witnesses note the loose thy shoe: the command of reverence. The witnesses note the the place is holy: from his presence. The witnesses note the the same as at the bush: the same divine Person (Exod. 3:5). The witnesses note the Joshua did so: the reverence due him. The expositors observe the loose thy shoe: the command of reverence. The expositors observe the the place is holy: from his presence. The expositors observe the the same as at the bush: the same divine Person (Exod. 3:5). The expositors observe the Joshua did so: the reverence due him. The believer is taught to mark the loose thy shoe, the the place is holy, the the same as at the bush, the Joshua did so, and to worship the Captain of the LORD's host. The verse thus records that the Captain bade Joshua loose his shoe, for the place was holy — the same word as at the bush, marking him the same divine Person, Christ, wherever present making holy ground (Exod. 3:5).
